description: 10 mandatory lab programs for the ACP course. All students must complete every program. Each program maps to an ACP Course Outcome (CO) and is graded on the 10-point rubric.
department: School of Computer Science and Engineering
course: Advanced C Programming with Generative AI

FUNC_MOD.name: Modular Programming with Functions
FUNC_MOD.description: Functions, call by value, call by reference, passing arrays and structures to functions.

exercises.title: Find maximum value and its index in a float array
exercises.concept_under_test: modular design, passing arrays to functions, for loop, if statement
exercises.problem_statement: Write a modular C program to read n float values from the user, store them in an array, find the maximum value and its index, and display the result. Each logical task (input, processing, output) must be a separate function.

test_cases.label: standard 5 floats
test_cases.expected_output:
Maximum value: 4.80
Index: 2

test_cases.label: single element
test_cases.expected_output:
Maximum value: 7.50
Index: 0

test_cases.label: all equal
test_cases.expected_output:
Maximum value: 2.00
Index: 0

test_cases.label: maximum at end
test_cases.expected_output:
Maximum value: 9.90
Index: 3

constraints: Must have at least 3 functions: input(), find_max_index(), output()
Array must be passed to functions (not global)
n must be read from the user (not hardcoded)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain how an array decays to a pointer when passed to a function
Can state why the function needs both the array and its size n
Can trace find_max_index() for the first two elements to verify logic

common_mistakes.mistake: Comparing arr[i] > arr[max_index] but initialising max_index to 0 — correct initialisation but wrong for edge case of all equal
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If all values are equal, what index does your function return? Is that the expected first occurrence?

common_mistakes.mistake: Not passing n to find_max_index — function doesn't know array bounds
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Inside find_max_index, how does the function know when to stop iterating if n is not passed?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
Meaningful variable names
exercises.grading_notes: Modular decomposition is the primary criterion. Output format must match exactly.

exercises.title: Compare areas of three rectangles using functions and structure
exercises.concept_under_test: struct definition, struct as function parameter, call by value, else-if ladder
function_prototypes: void compare_areas(Rectangle r1, Rectangle r2, Rectangle r3);
exercises.sample_output:
Area of Rect1: 15.00
Area of Rect2: 24.00
Area of Rect3: 16.00
Largest rectangle: Rect2 with area 24.00

test_cases.label: standard
test_cases.expected_output:
Area of Rect1: 15.00
Area of Rect2: 24.00
Area of Rect3: 16.00
Largest rectangle: Rect2 with area 24.00

test_cases.label: r3 largest
test_cases.expected_output:
Area of A: 1.00
Area of B: 4.00
Area of C: 25.00
Largest rectangle: C with area 25.00

constraints: Use typedef struct as specified
calculate_area() must take Rectangle by value (call by value)
compare_areas() must use an else-if ladder (not a loop)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what happens to the struct copy when passed by value to calculate_area()
Can state why area is a field in Rectangle rather than a local variable in main

common_mistakes.mistake: Sets area inside calculate_area but struct is passed by value — the change is not reflected in main
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: When you call calculate_area(rect), does the function receive rect itself or a copy? If it modifies rect.area inside, does that change the original in main?

common_mistakes.mistake: Else-if ladder has wrong condition order
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If Rect1 and Rect2 have equal areas, which branch does your else-if enter? Is Rect1 or Rect2 reported as largest? Is that what the spec requires?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
typedef must be declared before main
exercises.grading_notes: Call-by-value for structs is the key teaching point. Calculate_area should return the area, not modify the struct.

ARRAYSTRUCT.name: Array of Structures
ARRAYSTRUCT.description: Passing arrays of structures to functions, accessing structure members, modular programs using array of structures.

exercises.title: Array of Rectangle structures — area and largest
exercises.concept_under_test: array of structures, passing array of structures to functions, for loop
exercises.sample_output:
Rectangle 1: Area = 15.00
Rectangle 2: Area = 24.00
Rectangle 3: Area = 16.00
Largest rectangle: Rectangle 2 with area 24.00

test_cases.label: 3 rectangles
test_cases.expected_output:
Rectangle 1: Area = 15.00
Rectangle 2: Area = 24.00
Rectangle 3: Area = 16.00
Largest rectangle: Rectangle 2 with area 24.00

test_cases.label: single
test_cases.expected_output:
Rectangle 1: Area = 21.00
Largest rectangle: Rectangle 1 with area 21.00

test_cases.label: all equal
test_cases.expected_output:
Rectangle 1: Area = 16.00
Rectangle 2: Area = 16.00
Largest rectangle: Rectangle 1 with area 16.00

constraints: Array of Rectangle must be declared in main and passed to functions
calculate_area() fills the area field for all elements
findLargestArea() returns the index (0-based) of the largest element
n must be entered by the user
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state how to access rects[i].length inside a function
Can explain why calculate_area must modify the area field (not just return a value)
Can trace findLargestArea() for the test case showing which comparisons are made

common_mistakes.mistake: calculate_area passes struct by value — area field update lost after function returns
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: When rects is passed to calculate_area, is each element passed by value or by reference? What does that mean for the area field you write inside the function?

common_mistakes.mistake: findLargestArea initialises max_idx to -1 and crashes on empty array
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is the safest initial value for max_idx if n is guaranteed to be at least 1?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
typedef declared before main
exercises.grading_notes: Passing array of structs to functions is the core concept. Array elements are passed by reference (pointer to first element).

exercises.title: Flight search by destination using array of structures
exercises.concept_under_test: array of structures, string comparison, search function, passing array of structs
function_prototypes: void searchByDestination(int n, Flight_t f[], char searchDest[]);
exercises.sample_input:
AI101 Delhi 50
AI202 Mumbai 30
AI303 Bangalore 20
AI404 Chennai 10
Mumbai
exercises.sample_output: Flight found: AI202 to Mumbai with 30 available seats

test_cases.label: found
test_cases.input:
AI101 Delhi 50
AI202 Mumbai 30
AI303 Bangalore 20
AI404 Chennai 10
Mumbai
test_cases.expected_output: Flight found: AI202 to Mumbai with 30 available seats

test_cases.label: not found
test_cases.input:
AI101 Delhi 50
AI202 Mumbai 30
AI303 Bangalore 20
AI404 Chennai 10
Hyderabad
test_cases.expected_output: No flight found to Hyderabad

constraints: Array size is fixed at 4
Use strcmp() for destination comparison
searchByDestination() must print the result (not return it)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why == cannot be used to compare strings in C
Can state what strcmp returns for equal strings vs. different strings
Can trace searchByDestination for 'Mumbai' showing which comparisons are made

common_mistakes.mistake: Using == to compare strings instead of strcmp()
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does f[i].destination == searchDest compare in C? What do the two sides of == hold when destination is a char array?

common_mistakes.mistake: Loop continues after finding match — prints multiple results
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Once a matching flight is found, should the function continue checking remaining flights? What happens if two flights serve the same destination?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
#include <string.h> required for strcmp
exercises.grading_notes: String comparison with strcmp is the key correctness point. Early exit on match is required for efficiency marks.

PTR.name: Pointers
PTR.description: Pointer operations, passing arrays by reference, swap using pointers.

exercises.title: Swap two integer arrays using pointers
exercises.concept_under_test: passing arrays to functions by reference, pointer parameters, element-wise swap
function_prototypes: void swap_array(int n, int a[n], int b[n]);
exercises.problem_statement: Write a program to swap two integer arrays of the same size n element by element. The swap must be done inside a function using pointer parameters. After the swap, print both arrays.
exercises.sample_output:
Array A after swap: 4 5 6
Array B after swap: 1 2 3

test_cases.label: size 3
test_cases.expected_output:
Array A after swap: 4 5 6
Array B after swap: 1 2 3

test_cases.label: size 1
test_cases.expected_output:
Array A after swap: 20
Array B after swap: 10

test_cases.label: equal vals
test_cases.expected_output:
Array A after swap: 5 5
Array B after swap: 5 5

constraints: swap_array() must perform the swap without creating a third full-size array
A temporary single int variable for element swap is allowed
Use pointer notation inside swap_array (a[i] or *(a+i) both acceptable)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why arrays passed to swap_array() are modifiable (they decay to pointers)
Can trace the swap of a[0] and b[0] step by step using a temp variable

common_mistakes.mistake: Tries to swap the array pointers themselves instead of elements — no visible change
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If you swap the pointer variables a and b inside the function, does that change what main's arrays contain? Why not?

common_mistakes.mistake: Creates a full second array as buffer — unnecessary
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: To swap two integers, how many temporary storage locations do you need? Does that change when swapping element by element?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
exercises.grading_notes: Element-wise swap with a single temp variable is required. Robustness: n=1 and equal arrays must work.

DYNMEM.name: Dynamic Memory Allocation
DYNMEM.description: malloc, calloc, realloc, free, dangling pointer prevention, memory leak avoidance.

exercises.title: Manage a dynamic integer array — create, initialise, display, safely delete
exercises.concept_under_test: malloc, NULL check, free, dangling pointer prevention via double pointer
exercises.problem_statement:
Develop a modular C program to manage an integer array using dynamic memory allocation. Implement:
- create_array(n): allocates n ints with malloc, checks for NULL, returns pointer.
- initialize_array(arr, n): fills array with values 1..n.
- print_array(arr, n): prints all elements.
- delete_array(&arr): frees memory and sets pointer to NULL to prevent dangling access.

Demonstrate that arr is NULL after delete_array.
exercises.sample_output:
Array: 1 2 3 4 5
Array successfully deleted. Pointer is now NULL.

test_cases.label: n=5
test_cases.expected_output:
Array: 1 2 3 4 5
Array successfully deleted. Pointer is now NULL.

test_cases.label: n=1
test_cases.expected_output:
Array: 1
Array successfully deleted. Pointer is now NULL.

constraints: create_array() must return NULL if malloc fails and print an error
delete_array() must take int** (pointer to pointer) and set *arr = NULL after free
No fixed-size arrays anywhere in the program
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why delete_array takes int** instead of int*
Can state what a dangling pointer is and how setting to NULL prevents accidental use
Can explain what happens if you call free() twice on the same pointer

common_mistakes.mistake: delete_array takes int* — sets local copy to NULL, original in main unchanged
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After your delete_array returns, is the pointer in main set to NULL? How would you verify? What would you need to pass to the function to modify main's pointer?

common_mistakes.mistake: No NULL check after malloc
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does malloc return if the system has no memory left? What does your program do with that return value?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
NULL check on malloc mandatory
exercises.grading_notes: Double-pointer parameter in delete_array is the key teaching point. NULL check is required for robustness marks.

STROP.name: String Operations with User-Defined Functions
STROP.description: Reading and manipulating strings using user-defined functions and pointers, without relying on library functions.

exercises.title: Compare two strings using a user-defined function
exercises.concept_under_test: char array traversal, lexicographic comparison, user-defined string functions
exercises.problem_statement:
Write a C program to compare two strings using a user-defined function — do not use strcmp(). The compareStrings() function must return:
   0  if both strings are equal
   1  if the first string is lexicographically greater
  -1  if the second string is lexicographically greater

test_cases.label: equal
test_cases.expected_output: Strings are equal

test_cases.label: first greater
test_cases.expected_output: First string is greater

test_cases.label: second greater
test_cases.expected_output: Second string is greater

test_cases.label: prefix shorter
test_cases.expected_output: Second string is greater

constraints: Do not use strcmp(), strncmp(), or any other string library comparison function
compareStrings() must traverse character by character
Handle strings of different lengths correctly
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what lexicographic comparison means (character by character, using ASCII values)
Can state what compareStrings should return when 'app' is compared to 'apple'
Can trace the loop for 'hello' vs 'hello' showing all comparisons

common_mistakes.mistake: Stops at end of shorter string without checking if longer string continues
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Your loop exits when str1[i] == '\0'. If str2 still has characters remaining, which string is greater? Does your return statement handle that case?

common_mistakes.mistake: Returns ASCII difference instead of -1/0/1
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does your function return for 'b' vs 'a'? The spec requires -1, 0, or 1 — is your return value always in that set?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
No string library functions
exercises.grading_notes: Handling unequal-length strings is the edge case. Return values must be exactly -1, 0, or 1.

exercises.title: Concatenate two strings using a user-defined function
exercises.concept_under_test: char pointer traversal, null terminator, string concatenation without strcat, while/do-while
exercises.problem_statement: Write a C program to concatenate two strings using a user-defined function — do not use strcat(). The concatenate_strings() function appends str2 to the end of str1. str1 must be declared large enough to hold both strings. Use pointer notation and while or do-while loops.

test_cases.label: standard
test_cases.expected_output: Concatenated string: Hello, World!

test_cases.label: empty second
test_cases.expected_output: Concatenated string: Test

test_cases.label: empty first
test_cases.expected_output: Concatenated string: Suffix

constraints: Do not use strcat() or strncat()
str1 must be declared with enough capacity (e.g., char str1[200])
Use while or do-while loop (not for)
Use pointer notation inside concatenate_strings()
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain how to advance str1 to point to its own null terminator before appending
Can state why the null terminator must be written at the end of the concatenated result
Can trace the pointer movements for str1 = 'Hi' and str2 = '!'

common_mistakes.mistake: Writes str2 over str1 from the beginning instead of from str1's null terminator
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Where should the first character of str2 be placed in str1's buffer? How do you find that position?

common_mistakes.mistake: Forgets to write '\0' at the end of the concatenated string
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After copying all of str2's characters into str1, what does the next byte in str1's buffer contain? Is that a valid string terminator?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
Pointer notation mandatory inside concatenate_strings
exercises.grading_notes: Advancing past str1's null terminator before copying is the core pointer operation. Null terminator at end is required for correctness.

FILE.name: File Handling
FILE.description: File handling with text and binary files: fopen, fclose, fprintf, fscanf, fwrite, fread, feof, ferror.

exercises.title: Student records in a text file using fprintf and fscanf
exercises.concept_under_test: fprintf, fscanf, fopen (text mode), fclose, struct array with file I/O
function_prototypes: void writeToTextFile(Student students[], int n, const char *filename);
exercises.sample_output:
Data written to file successfully.
Data read back from file:
Alice 1001 8.50
Bob 1002 9.10

test_cases.label: 2 students
test_cases.expected_output:
Data written to file successfully.
Data read back from file:
Alice 1001 8.50
Bob 1002 9.10

constraints: Must check fopen return value for NULL in both write and read functions
Must close the file in the write function before the read function opens it
writeToTextFile() must use fprintf()
readFromTextFile() must use fscanf() and return number of records read
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain the difference between fopen modes 'w', 'r', and 'a'
Can state why the file must be closed before being re-opened for reading
Can explain what happens if fopen returns NULL and the program continues

common_mistakes.mistake: Opens the file for writing in main and passes FILE* — correct approach but students often don't close before read
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After writeToTextFile() finishes, is the file closed? If the OS has buffered writes, will all data be in the file before fscanf reads it?

common_mistakes.mistake: No NULL check on fopen — program crashes on permission error
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does fopen return if the file cannot be created (e.g., no disk space)? What is the next line of your program — is it safe to call fprintf with that value?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
NULL checks on fopen in every function that opens a file
exercises.grading_notes: NULL check is required for robustness marks. File close before read-back is required for correctness.

exercises.title: Student records in a binary file using fwrite and fread
exercises.concept_under_test: fwrite, fread, fopen (binary mode), sizeof struct, binary vs text file
function_prototypes: void writeToBinaryFile(Student students[], int n, const char *filename);
exercises.problem_statement:
Write a modular C program to perform the following tasks using binary file handling:
i.  Read the details of n students (name, roll_no, gpa) from the user and store them in an array of structures.
ii. Write the array of structures to a binary file using fwrite().
iii. Read the binary file into a second array of structures using fread().
iv. Display the array of structures.

Use the same Student typedef as Program 9.
exercises.sample_output:
Data written to binary file successfully.
Data read back from binary file:
Alice 1001 8.50
Bob 1002 9.10

test_cases.label: 2 students
test_cases.expected_output:
Data written to binary file successfully.
Data read back from binary file:
Alice 1001 8.50
Bob 1002 9.10

constraints: Must open files in binary mode: 'wb' for writing, 'rb' for reading
fwrite() must write the entire array in one call: fwrite(students, sizeof(Student), n, fp)
fread() must read back all records and return the number of records read
NULL check on fopen in both functions
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain the difference between 'w' (text) and 'wb' (binary) mode
Can state what fwrite's return value represents
Can explain why binary files are not human-readable but are faster for structured data

common_mistakes.mistake: Uses 'w' instead of 'wb' — on Windows, text mode converts newlines and corrupts binary data
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does fopen mode 'w' do to newline characters on Windows? Why does that matter when writing struct data that may contain arbitrary byte patterns?

common_mistakes.mistake: Calls fwrite element by element in a loop instead of one call for the whole array
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: fwrite(students, sizeof(Student), n, fp) writes n elements at once. What does your version write per call? Is the result the same in the file?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
NULL checks on fopen mandatory
Binary mode must be used ('wb'/'rb')
exercises.grading_notes: Binary mode is mandatory — text mode will cause data corruption on some platforms. fwrite called on the whole array is the idiomatic approach.
